name: Jacksonville (Duval County), Florida
tagline: The consolidated City of Jacksonville / Duval County — the largest city by land area in the continental U.S. and Florida's most-populous city (~1M residents) — runs enterprise IT through the Information Technologies Division (ITD), with an AI-forward, Microsoft/Azure-centric modernization agenda under Mayor Donna Deegan.
overview: Jacksonville is a consolidated city-county government: since 1968 the City of Jacksonville and Duval County have operated as a single entity, making it Florida's most-populous city (roughly 1 million residents) and the largest city by land area in the continental United States. It runs under a strong-mayor structure — Mayor Donna Deegan is the elected chief executive who proposes the budget and runs operations, with a 19-member City Council as the legislative body. Central technology is delivered by the Information Technologies Division (ITD), which the city currently lists under its Intra-Governmental Services Department (alongside Procurement, Fleet, and the city's Call Center), and which handles centralized selection, procurement, operation, and maintenance of the city's information systems, applications, data center, network, and security; the CIO's charter historically references a Finance & Administration reporting line, so confirm the current org placement before escalating. The CIO/Chief of Information Technologies is Wanyonyi Kendrick, who previously held IT leadership roles at Miami-Dade County and JEA. Jacksonville has positioned itself as a digital-government leader — it has ranked highly in the Center for Digital Government's Digital Cities survey for large cities and has been recognized by Microsoft for transparency dashboards built on Power BI and Azure — and is piloting AI in its budget process. For a Dell SLG seller, Jacksonville is a marquee local-government account: a large, modernization-minded consolidated buyer where the budget is adopted annually by Council and large IT purchases route through the city's Procurement Division.

budget.intro: Jacksonville's budget covers the consolidated city-county. Mayor Deegan's FY2025-26 budget was a roughly $2B operating plan (about a $110M increase over the prior year), holding the millage rate flat at 11.31 mills and avoiding any drawdown of reserves, paired with a $687M first-year Capital Improvement Plan within the FY2026-2030 CIP. IT is funded within departmental and capital lines and ITD's internal-service charges rather than a single published 'IT budget' figure.

stats.label: CIP first-year (FY26)
stats.note: First-year capital within the adopted FY2026-2030 Capital Improvement Plan

stats.label: Millage rate (mills)
stats.note: Held flat in FY25-26; no property-tax-rate increase

notes: There is no single public 'IT budget' line; technology spend is distributed across departmental operating budgets, the multi-year CIP, and ITD's internal-service charges.
The FY25-26 budget grew without a tax-rate increase or reserve drawdown, helped by new-construction property value and a one-time JEA contribution — expect continued emphasis on efficiency and value.
Budgets are proposed by the Mayor and adopted annually by the 19-member City Council, typically finalized in the fall.

initiatives.name: AI in the budget process
initiatives.summary: Jacksonville has piloted an enterprise AI program (reported with C3.ai on Microsoft Azure) to analyze department budgets — flagging spending duplications, inefficiencies, and forecasting opportunities across Public Works, Public Libraries, and Parks/Recreation. Early appetite for AI creates pull-through for AI-ready infrastructure, data platforms, and governance.

initiatives.name: Digital-government & transparency platforms
initiatives.summary: ITD has built nationally recognized transparency dashboards on Microsoft Power BI/Azure and relaunched the city website. These cloud-and-data programs anchor ongoing compute, storage, and analytics needs and underpin the city's strong Digital Cities survey showing.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity program
initiatives.summary: ITD runs the city-county's information-security program protecting consolidated systems and resident data. With Florida local governments a persistent ransomware target, this is a recurring buying center for endpoint, data protection, identity, resilience, and recovery.

initiatives.name: Data-center & infrastructure operations
initiatives.summary: ITD operates the city's centralized data center, network, and enterprise applications and is steadily modernizing toward hybrid cloud and shared services — a fit for infrastructure refresh, as-a-service, and managed offerings.

procurement.intro: Large Jacksonville IT buys route through the city's Procurement Division (grouped with ITD under the Intra-Governmental Services Department), which solicits, awards, and manages contracts for the consolidated city-county under Chapter 126 of the Ordinance Code. Vendors register through the city's e-procurement supplier portal, and the city can use and offer cooperative ('piggyback') contracts. Confirm Dell's standing on city contracts and on State of Florida / national co-op vehicles before forecasting.

vehicles.name: City of Jacksonville Procurement Division
vehicles.detail: Central acquisition organization (now listed under the Intra-Governmental Services Department); issues solicitations, awards city contracts, and runs the e-procurement supplier-registration portal. The primary path for competed buys.

vehicles.name: Cooperative / piggyback contracts
vehicles.detail: The Chief of Procurement may use cooperative contracts where practicable and advantageous, and the city's own competed contracts can be piggybacked by other governmental entities — a faster route than a fresh RFP.

vehicles.name: Florida state & national cooperatives
vehicles.detail: Florida local governments commonly leverage State of Florida contracts and national co-ops (e.g., NASPO ValuePoint, Sourcewell, OMNIA Partners) — confirm Dell coverage as alternative access paths.

powerStructure.name: Donna Deegan
powerStructure.title: Mayor (elected chief executive)
powerStructure.note: Strong mayor who proposes the budget and runs operations; sets the modernization and AI-pilot agenda. The top executive sponsor for major city technology direction.

powerStructure.name: Wanyonyi Kendrick
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Officer / Chief of Information Technologies (ITD)
powerStructure.note: Leads the Information Technologies Division; prior IT leadership at Miami-Dade County and JEA. The primary technology relationship for enterprise, cloud, AI, and infrastructure conversations.

powerStructure.name: Information Technologies Division (ITD)
powerStructure.title: Central IT (Intra-Governmental Services Department)
powerStructure.note: Owns enterprise applications, data center, network, and security for the consolidated city-county — the core buying center for infrastructure and as-a-service. The city now lists ITD under Intra-Governmental Services (with Procurement, Fleet, and the Call Center); confirm the current org line.

powerStructure.name: Procurement Division
powerStructure.title: Central procurement (Intra-Governmental Services)
powerStructure.note: Awards and manages city contracts and cooperative agreements; owns vendor registration and solicitations — the path onto city vehicles. Now grouped with ITD under Intra-Governmental Services.

powerStructure.name: Jacksonville City Council
powerStructure.title: 19-member legislative body
powerStructure.note: Adopts the annual budget and approves large contracts; district members influence priorities and procurement awards.

news.title: Statewide property-tax amendment could cost Jacksonville ~$300M/year starting FY27-28
news.impact: A November 2026 constitutional amendment raising the homestead exemption toward $250K (phasing in from 2027) could strip $300M+ in annual revenue by FY27-28 per the Council Auditor — expect intensified pressure on efficiency, consolidation, and value-priced vehicles; lead with refresh economics and as-a-service to protect budgets.

news.title: FY26-27 budget cycle underway with record public-safety asks (JSO ~$675.9M request)
news.impact: The Sheriff's Office requested ~$675.9M (up 5.9%) and Fire/Rescue ~$396.4M, driven by personnel costs; Mayor Deegan's budget address is expected in July with Council hearings in the fall. Public-safety tech (body-worn cameras, ShotSpotter) is in scope — position infrastructure, data, and security around a tightening budget.

news.title: Mayor Deegan unveils ~$2B FY25-26 budget with flat millage and no reserve drawdown
news.impact: Growth without a tax-rate increase signals emphasis on efficiency and value — lead with consolidation, refresh economics, and competitively priced contract vehicles.

news.title: Jacksonville pilots AI (reported with C3.ai on Azure) to analyze department budgets
news.impact: Executive appetite for AI creates pull-through for AI-ready infrastructure, data platforms, and governance/security.

news.title: Jacksonville ranks among the nation's top large cities in the Center for Digital Government's Digital Cities survey
news.impact: Strong digital-government posture validates a modernization-minded buyer; confirm the latest year's exact placement.

dellActions: Confirm Dell's standing on City of Jacksonville Procurement Division contracts and on State of Florida / national co-op vehicles (NASPO ValuePoint, Sourcewell, OMNIA) as the access paths to large buys.
Build the primary relationship with CIO Wanyonyi Kendrick and the ITD around enterprise infrastructure, data center, and hybrid-cloud modernization.

Lead with AI-ready infrastructure (compute, storage, data platforms) tied to the city's AI budget pilot and its Azure/Power BI analytics footprint — Dell as the on-prem/hybrid complement to its cloud stack.
Develop the security motion around endpoint, data protection, identity, and cyber-resilience for the consolidated city-county, given the elevated ransomware threat to Florida local governments.
Frame infrastructure refresh and as-a-service around efficiency and value, aligned to a budget that grew without a tax-rate increase, and engage Procurement early on the right contract vehicle.
